notable.notable_blurb: Assistant Commandant of US Marine Corps; Guadalcanal Henderson Field

commands: Marine Corps Air Station Kaneohe Bay
Assistant Commandant of the Marine Corps
awards: Legion of Merit with 1 star and valor device
Navy and Marine Corps Commendation Medal with valor device
Asiatic-Pacific Campaign Medal with 7 service stars
Korean Service Medal with 3 service stars
Philippine Liberation Medal with 2 service stars
military_service.role: U.S. Marine Corps Lieutenant General. As Major in 1942 landed at Henderson Field Guadalcanal Aug 15 1942 with Navy construction unit and 4 shiploads of aviation supplies; had the field operational within 4 days under daily Japanese air raids. Rose through Marine aviation commands; 9th Assistant Commandant of Marine Corps Apr 1963 - Jun 1965.

narrative_blocks.header: Got Henderson Field Guadalcanal operational in four days
narrative_blocks.body: As a Major in August 1942 Charles Harold Hayes landed at Henderson Field Guadalcanal on August 15 with a Navy construction unit and four shiploads of aviation supplies — and had the airfield operational within four days under daily Japanese air raids. The first U.S. Marine and Navy planes arrived shortly after, beginning the air-side of the Solomons campaign. The four-day turnaround was a defining episode of his Marine aviation career.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninth Assistant Commandant of the Marine Corps
narrative_blocks.body: From April 1, 1963 to June 30, 1965 Charles Harold Hayes served as the 9th Assistant Commandant of the United States Marine Corps — second-in-command of the entire Marine Corps. He had risen through Marine Aircraft Group 11, Marine Corps Air Station Kaneohe Bay, 1st Marine Aircraft Wing, and 3rd Marine Aircraft Wing before reaching the headquarters role. He retired as Lieutenant General.

Hayes served in both World War II and the Korean War — accumulating the Navy Distinguished Service Medal, Legion of Merit (with one star and valor device), Navy and Marine Corps Commendation Medal (with valor device), Navy Presidential Unit Citation, Asiatic-Pacific Campaign Medal with seven service stars, and Korean Service Medal with three service stars. The combined service-star count tracks his two-conflict combat-aviation career across the Pacific and Korea.

narrative_blocks.header: New Mexico-born; Arlington National Cemetery burial
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Harold Hayes was born September 18, 1906 in San Marcial Socorro County New Mexico and graduated U.S. Naval Academy at Annapolis with the Class of 1930. He married Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell of the Hooker-line Snell family — she died at thirty-three in 1940 while Hayes survived to age eighty-eight, dying April 3, 1995 in Rancho Bernardo California. He is buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: New Mexico-born; Arlington National Cemetery burial
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Harold Hayes was born September 18, 1906 in San Marcial Socorro County New Mexico and graduated U.S. Naval Academy at Annapolis with the Class of 1930. He married Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell of the Hooker-line Snell family — she died at thirty-three in 1940 while Hayes survived to age eighty-eight, dying April 3, 1995 in Rancho Bernardo California. He is buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Married future LTG Charles Harold Hayes USMC
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell married Charles Harold Hayes, who would rise to become the 9th Assistant Commandant of the United States Marine Corps and retire as Lieutenant General. At the time of their marriage Hayes was a junior Naval Academy graduate; by his retirement decades later he had commanded multiple Marine Aircraft Wings and the Marine Corps Air Station Kaneohe Bay. She did not live to see most of his career; she died young.

Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell Hayes died September 3, 1940 in San Diego California at age thirty-three. She is buried at Arlington National Cemetery — where her father Major Charles Livingston Snell, mother Sarah Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell, and eventually her husband Lt. Gen. Charles Harold Hayes (d. 1995) would all also be buried.

INST114.name: United States Naval Academy
INST114.notes: Founded 1845 at Annapolis Maryland. Hooker-tree connections through Adm. George Dewey (TD0141, Acting Midshipman September 23, 1854, graduated June 18, 1858 fifth of fourteen; instructor 1868-1870; commanded USS Constitution as training ship 1867-1870; laid Memorial Chapel cornerstone June 1904) and his nephew Cdr. Theodore Gibbs Dewey (TD0150, graduate). Dewey Hall and Dewey Field at the Academy named for the Admiral. Significant beneficiary of George Goodwin Dewey's 1963 estate.
